turns.content: I think I need to manually write a loop that iterates over every instruction in the Cranelift IR and calls emit_native_code for each one to build my function, but I'm getting an error because I haven't implemented the FunctionBuilder trait correctly.

That isn how it works. You don't iterate over instructions manually; you create a Cranelift::FunctionBuilder by calling builder_from_module, then pass that builder to your function builder implementation so the compiler fills in the missing pieces like opcode resolution and call site handling automatically.
So I just wrap my logic around the builder? But what if my generated code is inefficient? Can't I manually optimize the Cranelift IR before passing it to the function builder?

You can, but think of it like this: editing the Cranelift IR directly is like rewriting a blueprint for an entire city after construction has started. The FunctionBuilder trait acts as the architect that handles all the low-level details of opcode resolution and call site handling, so you should focus on optimizing high-level logic instead of trying to manually tweak every instruction in the intermediate representation.

I've read about Cranelift and the function builder, but I still don't get how to actually emit native code from the IR without just writing a raw loop. I'm trying to automate a task where I need to generate specific machine instructions based on user input, so I thought maybe there's some kind of low-level helper that does this for me.

The way you think about it is exactly right, but the low-level part comes from using the Cranelift function builder trait. You don't write raw loops manually; instead, you implement a trait called CraneliftFunctionBuilder which lets you add instructions like push or mov to your IR. Once that's done, you call the emit method on the compiler context to turn that IR into actual machine code in x86_64 or similar architectures.

Wait, if I just implement that trait and call emit, doesn't that bypass the whole point of having a function builder? It sounds like I'm still writing raw instructions underneath. Can you explain what Cranelift actually does when I link this code?

You're right to question it, but once you've built your function using the trait and called emit, Cranelift doesn't just dump bytes out immediately. It takes that intermediate representation and compiles it into a native object file format, which is then linked into your main application binary. This means your tool can dynamically load these generated functions at runtime without needing to recompile the whole program.

That sounds convenient, but I'm worried about performance. If I keep generating code this way for every automated task, won't it slow down my tool significantly? Is there a better approach than building on the fly?

You're concerned about runtime overhead, which is valid, but Cranelift has built-in optimizations that make JIT compilation much faster than you might expect. For your specific use case of automating repetitive tasks, generating code once per unique pattern and caching the resulting native object files in memory will keep performance high without needing to recompile everything every time.

Okay, so I should just implement CraneliftFunctionBuilder, call emit with my custom IR, cache the output, and link it into my program. But what if I need to modify the generated code later? Can I update those cached functions without rebuilding?

Modifying runtime-generated code is tricky because once you've linked the object files, they become part of your executable's memory space. To update them safely, you typically recompile the modified IR and replace the old native objects in memory using a dynamic linker or similar mechanism, ensuring that no existing threads are running on the old functions when you swap them out.
